author: @tonyhardy-kp4os
text:
Technically it is called an electrical generator not an energy generator. I get what you are saying about how to think about energy transformation, good talk thank you.. But the engineers and scientists labeling that thing as an electrical generator sounds legitimate because it indeed generates electricity when otherwise you would have none from just spinning a wheel with no coils and no magnetic field. You could spin that wheel all night long but without that electrical generation happening using those coils, magnetic field, and you spinning the wheel you will get no electricity out.  Put it this way, try hooking the input up to nothing and see if it creates electricity. lol

It is not a big deal, just a misunderstanding. Too many people been running around calling it simply a generator and leaving out the first word that goes along with it to describe it correctly, and that is the word "electrical" NOT the word "energy". Engineers and scientists know it is an electrical generator, but many people)(mostly people not scientists or engineers, etc...) are just too lazy to say it correctly and they shorten it to  just "generator".  And now it can be a little confusing to others who are not educated in the field hearing regular people say it leaving that detail out.

There are many different types of generation that happens here, just saying only the word "generation" requires more information if you want to know what they are trying to say. The first question is "generate WHAT?".

author: @michaelward402
text:
I wouldn't call it a true converter either, which to me, takes something in and converts it to something else with no losses. A generator, by its name, makes no assumption about the input or what the expectations are regarding its efficiency.
A generator, or converter, as you want to call it, can take a form of energy input and utilise that energy to generate another form of energy. That it has losses due to many things, marks it as a generator and not a converter.

author: @vidard9863
text:
the problem is that the "generator is generally the bookend of the conversation. 
when we discuss electricity the generator is the thing that generates the current, and that is the end of the conversation.
when we, in very small groups, plan a power plant, the "turbine" is the end goal, we don't really care what happens after the "generator", and it is rarely called a generator, it consumes the power.
that is why car alternators are almost never called generators, alternators are not a book end, they are in the middle of any concept of the cars functions that include it.

author: @maukaman
text: Apparently a lot of people have a hard time understanding the innate limitations of energy conversion because I often find myself trying to explain to people that there would be no benefit to adding a dynamo or alternator to my electric dirtbike, other than in the case of regenerative braking of course.  My neighbor kept insisting that I attach a dc motor he gave me to be spun with the friction of my back wheel.  After spending almost an hour trying to explain the concept for him I finally rigged it up like he had suggested and wired his motor to power the big LED headlight already on the bike, with a switch before the light.  I had him ride it and told him to test the bike out with the light switched off then on.  He finally started to understand the concept once he could feel the drag induced from that motor when it was powering the headlight.  Oh the things I will do to win an argument lol 😂

author: @flyingbeaver57
text:
As always, thank you for this excellent way of describing, the forms, functions, components and "parameters" (not a favorite word, lol). Just a footnote: some time ago, I was playing with a motor that had been re-purposed, as you've done. One part of making it all work well was going from a low RPM "source" to drive a shaft at high RPM. I had no pieces on hand that fit the job, so it was off to the Library (back in the pre-Internet days) and "Machinery's Handbook."  (The term "handbook" was misleading for a book that was 2 kg & 1400+ pages!).
Probably the most interesting bit of information turned up as a "side note" to the job was the choice between gears, belts & pulleys, and roller chains & sprockets. What came out when comparing the alternatives was the efficiency of a roller chain and sprocket transmission vs. a gear drive. Even with well-made and matched gears, properly lubricated, the energy lost between the rotating source and the driven shaft was much greater than if roller chain & sprockets (at the same speed-change ratio) were used.

Belts & pulleys were in the middle, but roller chains are amazing efficient in many cases, compared to a geared transmission. Of course there are trade-offs depending on the application, but in some cases worth a look. 
One very good source of roller chain system parts (aside from old bicycles) is a farm machinery supplier. I've found that many combinations of parts to move rotation energy, with or without a speed change, are  readily available than gear sets. In some cases, Gears are wonderful, but roller chain drives are quiet, efficient, and often less costly.

author: @WalterBurton
text:
It's the usual problem with overloaded terms and context. I'm sure the term has ... generated ... plenty of confusion through the ages. But hey, at least we don't call them "creators." :-) 

The reason "converter" doesn't work is because what you're really saying is "electrical converter" or "electricity converter," and, well, you see the issue, I'm sure. 

I'm afraid "generator" is here to stay, but of course your critique is 100% correct, and understanding that a generator doesn't actually create anything (in a humanities, _de novo_ sense) is important.

EDIT: When I say, "here to stay," I might be being a bit overly conservative. I can imagine a new term gaining traction, maybe even within our lifetimes. Perhaps it's time.

author: @C-M-E
text:
This hits the point on the long, convoluted bevy of terms we as humans have accrued when applying a naming convention to our ideas. I've always seen a generator as a transformer in name and function, but of course someone long enough ago coined that title to another device, thus nullifying my efforts. 
Speaking of bass ackwards, I was watching a 'misconceptions of electricity' type of video the other day trying to sway hearts and minds that electrons don't flow through copper wires via current, but instead flow outside the wires as EM fields along the lines in a gigantic loop between the power station and your house. 
Seemed interesting, but I didn't see the thought train they were trying to push. There's an awful lot with the idea that anyone who's worked with electrical lines would raise a hand of contention.

author: @Jakeup211
text:
Rob even though you induce mechanical torque on the rotor. 
Is it not the moving magnetic field the power is flowing from and not the actual rotational torque, but the rotating magnetic field??

Because motors can spin without mechanical rotational input, with electric fields causing the magnetic field to induce rotation.

replies.author: @Jakeup211
replies.text:
Basically a moving magnetic field can induce current to flow. 
But that means with the right configuration of transformers we should be able to create a large simulated virtual rotating magnetic field and power a load then send it right back into the rotating magnetic field. 

Only by using resistive losses to power a load. 
The amps would be almost free or recycled from and back to the magnetic field flowing in time.

author: @SpellsOfTruth
text:
Since 'generators' are effected by strength of field, speed of rotation, and length of wire. Wouldn't it be possible to take a motor and a 'generator', then give the motor a massively large gear or pulley ratio and connect it to the 'generator'. The gear ratio will drastically increase the rotation speed of the generator enough so that the motors power source could be switched to the generator and there would be power left over to plug in other devices. This is wouldn't ever be done when dealing with just motors, because a large gear ratio would mean a very low torque for the faster gear/pulley end, but torque doesn't matter when it comes to 'generators'.

EDIT: LOL I spoke too soon, you basically say what I said at 13:28 haha.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Power is the amount of energy transferred or converted per unit time.  One motor can not have more power than another, it can only do ‘work’ faster. The consumption of this energy being defined by volts times amps per time. Be less confusing to say that this generator in question converts mechanical force into electrical potential. If that potential can be provided with a suitable path then energy can be transferred and manifest a force somewhere else of one’s choosing. The force can liberate electrons from static atoms as in a plate of a say battery or electrode of an arc welder or ionizer or cathode of a cathode ray tube...etc. The magnetic gradient (field) around the path can also be used to manifest mechanical force in say a motor or solenoid or send protons wizzing around a particle accelerator.  I’ll leave out the manifestations of photons and what not.
